title: RSI (Rapid Sequence Intubation)
content_markdown:
# RSI (Rapid Sequence Intubation)

**This is an ICP only CMG.** All intubations (excluding patients in cardiac arrest) will follow this procedure.

#### Indications
- The patient with unequivocal life-threatening airway compromise and clinical evidence of severe hypoxia, where the airway cannot be managed by less aggressive means.
**or**- The patient with potential airway compromise where extrication will make it impossible to maintain adequate airway control, or where the airway cannot be managed by less aggressive means and where time to hospital (including extrication, load and transport times) is more than 10 minutes. 

#### Considerations in Applying Indications
The following must be considered prior to utilisation of this guideline: 

- Response to basic treatment: 
- Ability to oxygenate, ventilate and protect the airway by less aggressive means.

- Remember the primary goals of airway management.
- Consider the risks and benefits of intubation.
- Clinical needs are influenced by the following: 
- Clear and obvious airway compromise
- Significantly decreased level of consciousness (GCS 2 Caution:
- Hypothermic patient
- Fitting patient- Patient with reversible pathology.
#### Procedure
- **Basic airway management**
- Posture, suction, oral/nasal airway, LMA.

- **Oxygenation**
- Aim for the highest SpO2 by the most efficient method
- High flow oxygen by nasal prongs is mandatory throughout this procedure.
- **IV / IO access**
- Establish a fast-flowing line that is reliable and secure 
- A second line is desirable.##### Prepare Patient
- Correct any hypotension / hypovolaemia prior to / concurrently with this procedure 
- 10ml/kg normal saline unless in pulmonary oedema.

- Pre‐oxygenate.
- Monitor patient: 
- ECG
- SpO2- EtCO2.
- Execute CMG 3B Intubation Algorithm.
#### Drug Sequence
- **Adult:**
- Ketamine: 1mg/kg fast push
- Suxamethonium: 1.5mg/kg over 30-60 seconds. 

- **Post intubation to maintain tube and level of sedation:**
**Infusion Pump**
- Morphine + Midazolam 
 Consider: Adjunct 0.05mg/kg bolus of morphine for patients with severely painful injuries to achieve adequate analgesia. 
 OR 
- Ketamine (preferred for patients at risk of shock)**Syringe boluses**
- Ketamine 
 Consider: Adjunct 0.05mg/kg boluses of midazolam with ketamine infusions/boluses to achieve adequate sedation.
- Suxamethonium may cause bradycardia 
- If patient is bradycardic once ETT is tied in, consider a dose of atropine.
- **If suxamethonium is contraindicated:**
- Reconsider the need for intubation
- If intubation is required, administer 2mg/ kg ketamine IV and attempt intubation.
**Note:** laryngospasm following ketamine is rare, but may occur. Also, intubation conditions will be less than ideal – consider carefully if other airway management options are appropriate.

#### If Intubation Fails
- Re‐oxygenate / re‐ventilate the patient 
- Utilise basic techniques.

- There is **no second dose** of suxamethonium.
- Execute CMG 3B Intubation Algorithm.
#### Follow Up
- All relevant details will be carefully documented on the PCR 
- This especially applies to details concerning the need for sedation, the checks on correct placement of the ETT and the results of the procedure.

- All additional RSI paperwork must be sent to CGU by the end of the shift. **There are no exceptions.**- All pharmacologically facilitated intubations will be subject to routine, mandatory review.\u2003

##### Preparation

- Adequate resources available 
- Skills mix
- Crew numbers

- Assign roles

- C-spine immobilisation required? 
- Yes / No

- Optimise patient’s position and airway

- Optimise environment and equipment

- Pre-oxygenate: nasal prongs with 15L/minute. 

- Sufficient oxygen available.

- Suction available.

- IV/IO access (x2 if possible).

- Monitoring: 
- ECG
- NIBP/BP
- SpO2- EtCO2##### Drugs

- IV fluids.

- RSI drugs drawn up: 
- Suxamethonium
- Ketamine

##### Equipment 

- Connect EtCO2 to monitor.

- OPA / NPA.

- BVM.

- Airtraq with tested loaded tube, switched ON.

- Syringe.

- ETT loaded with bougie.

- Laryngoscope checked.

- Securing method.

##### Team Brief

- External laryngeal manipulation discussed.

- Fallback plan discussed 
- LMA
- Scalpel for cric with 6mm ETT.

#### Post RSI Checklist

- Confirm ETT placement 
- EtCO2 (monitor or EMMA)
- Visualising the ETT pass the cords
- Auscultation
- Colleague check placement
- Misting in the tube- Chest rise and fall- Oxygen saturations
- Secure the tube 
- Thomas block
- Tube tie

- Reassessment 
- ABCs and VSS

- Sedation plan discussed

- Deterioration plan discussed

- Extrication and transport plan discussed

. If dilution of the drug is required, open the normal saline ampoule or insert the needle and syringe into the injection port of a bag of saline. Draw back on the syringe and draw up the required amount of fluid for dilution of the drug (i.e. 9mls of normal saline with 1ml of Morphine Sulphate for a total volume of 10mls).
Cannula (minimum 18G), bung, normal saline flush, tegaderm, ambulance cannula sticker and sharps container.

In the conscious/semi conscious patient, the port line should be primed with the approved designated dose of Lignocaine. This is to be flushed with 1.2-1.5mls of normal saline in order to push the Lignocaine into the medullary space. Wait 2 to 3mins for Lignocaine to take effect.
Always aspirate 5ml from the line and discard prior to first use. Flush the line after each dose with 10ml of normal saline.
Flush the line after each dose with 10ml of normal saline.

##### Type
Isotonic crystalloid solution of 0.9% sodium chloride. 
 Contains 151mMol sodium and 151mMol chloride per litre [no schedule] 

##### Presentations
500 or 1000ml of 0.9% sodium chloride solution

##### Actions

- Short acting plasma volume expander

- Also expands interstitial fluid volume

#### Uses

- Initial replacement of fluid, in volume depleted or dehydrated patients. (Volume depletion may be due to loss of blood, plasma or fluid and electrolytes)

- Maintenance of hydration during prolonged patient contact time

- To keep vein open, as IV route for drugs

#### Adverse Effects
Fluid overload 

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses##### Adult
- **In general, aim to maintain sBP ≥90mmHg: **rate and volume infused is dependent on patient condition. Give 250-500mL boluses, reassess after each bolus.
- **Haemorrhagic hypovolaemia: **aggressive warm fluids. 
- Max. dose: 20ml/kg

- **Traumatic brain injury: **aim for sBP ≥100mmHg 
- No limit on amount - dependent on condition of patient. 
- **TKVO: **10 drops per minute (30ml/h with standard drip set)##### Paediatric
- **Refer to paediatric reference charts for BP goals: **rate and volume infused is dependent on patient condition. Give weight appropriate boluses, reassess after each bolus.
- **Paediatric cardiac arrest: **20ml/kg- **Haemorrhagic hypovolaemia: **aggressive warm fluids 
- Max dose: 20ml/kg
- **TKVO: **10 drops per minute (30ml/h with standard drip set)

Morphine.text: Morphine: 10mg IV or SC Midazolam: 5mg IV or 10mg SC. Repeat @10/60 (max. dose 60mg)
Calculated dose of morphine above 10 mg Doses above 10 mg are rarely required.
Calculated dose of morphine above 20 mg
IV/IO morphine may be administered to patients with mild-moderate hypotension (sBP 70 – 90mmHg) 0.025mg/kg IV/IO over 60 seconds.
Withdraw 29mL from 50mL NaCl bag, then add 3mL (30mg) morphine and 6mL (30mg) midazolam to make 30mg:30mg/30mL
Consider: Adjunct 0.05mg/kg bolus of morphine for patients with severely painful injuries to achieve adequate analgesia.
Withdraw 29mL from 50mL NaCl bag, then add 6mL (30mg) midazolam and 3mL (30mg) morphine to make 30mg:30mg/30mL

. If dilution of the drug is required, open the normal saline ampoule or insert the needle and syringe into the injection port of a bag of saline. Draw back on the syringe and draw up the required amount of fluid for dilution of the drug (i.e. 9mls of normal saline with 1ml of Morphine Sulphate for a total volume of 10mls).

##### Type
Opioid analgesic [S8]

##### Presentations
10mg in 1ml – glass ampoule

##### Actions

- Analgesia

- Anxiolytic

- Vasodilation

##### Pharmacokinetics
**IV**
 Onset: 5 - 10 minutes
 Max Effect: 20 minutes
 Duration: 4 - 5 hours

**IM**
 Onset: 10 - 30 minutes
 Max Effect: 30 minutes
 Duration: 4 - 5 hours

#### Uses

- Analgesia for moderate to severe pain

- Maintain analgosedation in intubated patients in conjunction with midazolam.

- Reduce dyspnoea in palliative care patients.

- Relieve suffering due to terminal palliative care emergencies (no active treatment).

#### Adverse Effects

- Nausea and vomiting

- Altered level of consciousness

- Respiratory depression

- Hypotension

- Bradycardia

#### Contraindications

- Respiratory depression

- Systolic blood pressure Disregard contraindications when managing palliative care emergencies (no active treatment) patients.

#### Precautions

- GCS ≤13

- Known hypersensitivity

- Frail patients

- COPD patients

- Hypovolaemic patients

- Systolic BP 70 – 90mmHg (see below)

- Children **AP:** max. total dose 20mg
- **ICP:** no max. total dose
- Consider IM: 
- IV/IO unobtainable
- Little risk of hypotension- Patient contact estimated >20/60- No ischaemic chest pain- 0.1mg/kg IM 
- Repeat once @30/60 if required
- **AP:** max. total dose 20mg##### Adults only
IV/IO morphine may be administered to patients with mild-moderate hypotension (sBP 70 – 90mmHg)

- 0.025mg/kg IV/IO over 60 seconds. 
- Repeat @10/60 as required.
- Do not repeat if sBP drops ≥10mmHg, even if it remains above 70mmHg. 
- **AP:** max. total dose 20mg- **ICP:** no max. total dose#### Post-intubation analgosedation (as adjunct to midazolam)##### Adult (infusion pump)
**Note:** Withdraw 29mL from 50mL NaCl bag, then add 3mL (30mg) morphine and 6mL (30mg) midazolam to make 30mg:30mg/30mL

- **ICP: **Initial infusion rate: 5mg/hr 
- Lower soft infusion limit: 1mg/hr
- Upper soft infusion limit: 30mg/hr
- Upper hard infusion limit: 50mg/hr- **ICP: **Default bolus: 2mg (2mL) 
- Lower hard bolus limit: 0.5mg (0.5mL)
- Upper hard bolus limit: 5mg (5mL)#### Palliative care
**Note:** Always refer to and follow patient specific treatment plan when available.
##### Adult with dyspnoea
- 2.5 - 5mg SC/IM/IV
Consider patient weight when selecting dose
##### Adult with distressing pain
- Refer to Palliative Care Medication Calculator
##### Adult with terminal palliative care emergency
- SC/IM/IV: 10mg
In conjunction with midazolam
##### Paediatric
Refer to patient specific treatment plan for all palliative care presentations.

Withdraw 29mL from 50mL NaCl bag, then add 3mL (30mg) morphine and 6mL (30mg) midazolam to make 30mg:30mg/30mL
Up to 4mg/kg IM injection
Consider: Adjunct 0.05mg/kg boluses of midazolam with ketamine infusions/boluses to achieve adequate sedation.
It is permissible to concurrently administer Midazolam and Levetiracetam if a seizure has persisted ≥20/60 without any pharmacological management prior to ACTAS arrival.
If patient has previously had an unpleasant (fearful) experience with adenosine, consider a pre-dose of midazolam 1 – 1.5mg IV.
0.2mg/kg IM (max. 10mg)
Withdraw 29mL from 50mL NaCl bag, then add 6mL (30mg) midazolam and 3mL (30mg) morphine to make 30mg:30mg/30mL

##### Type
Benzodiazepine 

##### Presentations
15mg in 3mL ampoule

##### Actions

- Antiepileptic

- Sedative

- Anxiolytic

- Amnesic

##### Pharmacokinetics
**IV/IO**
 Onset: 2 minutes
 Max Effect: 3 - 5 minutes
 Duration: 7 - 75 minutes

**IM**
 Onset: 5 - 15 minutes
 Max Effect: 30 - 60 minutes
 Duration: 2 - 6 hours

#### Uses

- Status epilepticus

- Post intubation analgosedation in conjunction with morphine

- Relieve distress or restlessness in the palliative care patient

- Relieve suffering due to terminal palliative care emergencies (no active treatment)

#### Adverse Effects

- Hypotension
More common when administer via fast IV push

- Respiratory depression and loss of airway
More common at high doses or when synergising with other CNS depressant substances

#### Contraindications

- Previous allergic reaction to Midazolam

#### Precautions

- Hepatic failure

- Frail or elderly

- Known or suspected concurrent CNS depressant use

- Known or suspected haemodynamic compromise

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- **Approved** for administration in ACTAS setting.

##### Breastfeeding
- **Approved** for administration in ACTAS setting.

#### Doses#### Status Epilepticus##### Adult and Paediatric
- 0.2mg/kg IM (max. 10mg) 
- Repeat @5/60 if required. (max. 2 doses)

- **ICP:** 0.2mg/kg IV/IO push over 60 sec. (max. 10mg) 
- Repeat @5/60 if required. (max. 2 doses)
#### Post Intubation Sedation (as adjunct to ketamine)##### Adult and Paediatric
- **ICP:** 0.05mg/kg IV/IO push over 60 sec. 
- Repeat @5/60 as required.

#### Post Intubation Sedation (as adjunct to morphine)##### Adult
**Note:** Withdraw 29mL from 50mL NaCl bag, then add 6mL (30mg) midazolam and 3mL (30mg) morphine to make 30mg:30mg/30mL

- **ICP:** Initial infusion rate: 5mg/hr 
- Lower soft infusion limit: 1mg/hr
- Upper soft infusion limit: 30mg/hr
- Upper hard infusion limit: 50mg/hr- **ICP:** Default bolus: 2mg (2mL) 
- Lower hard bolus limit: 0.5mg (0.5mL)
- Upper hard bolus limit: 5mg (5mL)#### Palliative Care
**Note:** Always refer to and follow patient specific treatment plan when available.
##### Adult with distress or restlessness
- 2.5 – 5mg SC/IM
Consider patient weight when selecting dose
##### Adult with terminal palliative care emergency
- 5mg SC/IM/IV
In conjunction with morphine
- Repeat @10/60 if requires (max. 60mg)

##### Paediatric
Refer to patient specific treatment plan for all palliative care presentations.

Withdraw 14mL from 50mL NaCl bag, then add 4mL (400mg) ketamine to make 400mg/40mL
Ketamine should only be used as an analgesia adjunct if severe pain is unremitting 10 minutes post a second weight-based opioid dose.
0.3mg/kg Dilute to 10mL. Administer via Springfusor 0.15mg/kg Dilute to 10mL. Administer via Springfusor
Consider: Adjunct 0.05mg/kg boluses of midazolam with ketamine infusions/boluses to achieve adequate sedation.
If intubation is required, administer 2mg/ kg ketamine IV and attempt intubation.

##### Type
Anaesthetic Agent

- **High-risk medication**

##### Presentations
200mg in 2ml – vial 

50mg in 5ml - vial

##### Actions
Complex, multiple actions:

- Analgesic at low doses

- Sedative

- Amnestic/dissociative properties

- Has bronchodilating properties

Onset of action is rapid: IV = 1 – 2 min; IM = 4 – 8 min.

#### Uses

- Sedation of combative/agitated patients, who are an immediate danger to themselves or others

- Induction agent for intubation **- ICP only**

- Maintenance of sedation **- ICP only**

- Procedural dissociation to facilitate painful interventions (e.g., straightening long bones, extrication) **- ICP only**

- Analgesia for severe pain in the patient with signs of shock, or is at risk of shock **- ICP only**

- Analgesia adjunct for severe unremitting pain 10 minutes post second opioid dose

- Analgesia for severe pain in patients who are unable to have, or have developed a significant tolerance to opioids

#### Adverse Effects

- Laryngospasm

- Hypersalivation

- Dysphoria/emergence reactions

- Muscle twitching and purposeless movements

- Respiratory depression → apnoea (seen in extreme doses or fast administration)

- Hypotension (generally seen in hypovolaemic/cardiac depressed patients)

#### Contraindications

- Known sensitivity

- Active myocardial ischaemia or LVF or symptomatic hypertension (not applicable when used for RSI or to maintain sedation for ETT) 

- Children 65 years, or with general debility.
- Repeat 1mg/kg IM injection after 5 minutes (if required).

#### For intubation induction with Suxamethonium##### Adult only
- **ICP:** 1mg/kg IV/IO over 30-60 seconds 

#### For intubation when suxamethonium is contraindiated ##### Adult only
- **ICP:** 2mg/kg IV/IO over 30-60 seconds 

#### Post intubation analgosedation##### Adult and Paediatric (Syringe bolus)
- **ICP:** Up to 1mg/kg IV or IO 
- 1-5 minute intervals as required.

##### Adult (Infusion pump)
**Note:** Withdraw 14mL from 50mL NaCl bag, then add 4mL (400mg) ketamine to make 400mg/40mL 

- **ICP:** Initial infusion rate: 100mg/hr 
- Lower soft infusion limit: 10mg/hr 
- Upper soft infusion limit: 400mg/hr 
- Upper hard infusion limit: 800mg/hr 
- **ICP:** Default bolus: 20mg (2mL) 
- Lower hard bolus limit: 5mg (0.5mL)
- Upper hard bolus limit: 100mg (10mL)

#### Ketamine Analgesia Dose Chart
- Following mild-moderate analgesic options opioids should be considered prior to ketamine.
- Ketamine should only be used as an analgesia adjunct if severe pain is unremitting 10 minutes post a second weight-based opioid dose.
- Ketamine is a first line analgesic only in the setting of current or foreseen hypoperfusion; and in the patient unable to receive opioids due to either allergy, hypersensitivity, or tolerance.**Dose:******≥65 years old**
INITIAL IV

(ICP/AP)

0.3mg/kg

Dilute to 10mL. Administer via Springfusor

0.15mg/kg

Dilute to 10mL. Administer via Springfusor

REPEAT IV

(ICP/AP)

As initial

15 mins post end of initial infusion.

As initial

15 mins post end of initial infusion.

INITIAL IM

(ICP/AP)

0.5mg/kg

0.25mg/kg

REPEAT IM

(ICP/AP)

After 30 minutes:

0.5mg/kg

After 30 minutes:

0.25mg/kg

HYPOPERFUSED PATIENT

(ICP)

Halve above doses

Halve above doses

#### Special Notes
- Patients who have received ketamine may still have a significant awareness, despite an appearance of unconsciousness. They may be able to hear and have some recall. Patients should be advised that they will experience strange / unusual sensations.
- Ketamine is a Controlled Medicine. Use must be checked by both crew members. Under CL207 – Controlled Medicines Procedure, recording and accounting for its use is a legal requirement.
- The unused portion of the dose must be appropriately disposed of, and the disposal recorded.

Suxamethonium 100mg in 2ml Suxamethonium (applies to 100mg in 2ml):  40kg = 60 mg (1.2 ml) 50kg = 75 mg (1.5 ml) 60kg = 90 mg (1.8 ml) 70kg = 105 mg (2.1 ml) 80kg = 120 mg (2.4 ml) 90kg = 135 mg (2.7 ml) 100kg = 150 mg (3 ml) Maximum dose 150 mg
If heart rate less than 50/minute, consider atropine prior to suxamethonium.

##### Type
Depolarising muscle relaxant [S4]

- **High-risk medication**

##### Presentations
100mg in 2ml – plastic ampoule

##### Actions
Acts like the neurotransmitter acetylcholine at the neuromuscular junction. Persists for a period long enough to exhaust the motor endplate by prolonged depolarisation.

Onset IV: approx 45 seconds 
 Duration IV: 5 – 7 minutes 

#### Uses
To facilitate airway management in selected patients 

#### Adverse Effects

- Bradycardia

- Potassium release

- Increased intraocular and intragastric pressure

- Occasionally, prolonged paralysis

- Has been associated with malignant hyperthermia

#### Contraindications

- Previous reaction to suxamethonium

- Suspected hyperkalaemia

- Use in children

#### Precautions

- Elderly patient

- Neuromuscular disease

- Hypothermic patient

- Fitting patient

- Patient with reversible pathology

Select patients carefully – always have a fallback position!

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses##### Adult
- **ICP: **1.5mg/kg IV 
- Over 30 – 60 seconds
- To a maximum of 150mg.

##### Paediatric
Not used.

#### Suxamethonium 100mg in 2ml
- Suxamethonium (applies to 100mg in 2ml): 
- 40kg = 60 mg (1.2 ml)
- 50kg = 75 mg (1.5 ml)
- 60kg = 90 mg (1.8 ml)
- 70kg = 105 mg (2.1 ml)- 80kg = 120 mg (2.4 ml)- 90kg = 135 mg (2.7 ml)- 100kg = 150 mg (3 ml)- **Maximum dose 150 mg**
#### Special Notes
- To be used only following completion of the ACTAS designated training programme.
- If heart rate less than 50/minute, consider atropine prior to suxamethonium.
- Suxamethonium may cause bradycardia. If patient is bradycardic once ETT is tied in, consider a dose of atropine.
- **Note:** bradycardia may be a result of a head injury and raised ICP – thus, BP will be elevated. In this case there is no requirement for atropine regardless of the degree of bradycardia.
- Suxamethonium must be drawn up into a red barrelled syringe.

Treat with 100% oxygen.

##### Type
A naturally occurring gas 

##### Presentations
Compressed gas in all-white cylinders of various sizes, to be fitted with an appropriate pressure-reducing device 

##### Actions
Essential element for aerobic metabolic needs and sustaining life. 
 Oxygen administration improves, or prevents a reduction in, the oxygen content of blood leaving the lungs. 

#### Uses
A wide range of situations, for the treatment or prevention of hypoxaemia

#### Adverse Effects

- Increased risk of fire / explosion – oxygen strongly supports combustion 

- Mucosal dryness and irritation

- Hypoventilation in some COPD patients with CO2 retention (titrate oxygen to maintain SpO2 88 – 92%)

#### Contraindications

- Known paraquat poisoning with SpO2 ≥88%

- History of bleomycin therapy with SpO2 ≥88%

#### Precautions
Neonates (especially pre-term infants) are particularly susceptible to the toxic effects of oxygen. When absolutely required, the lowest effective concentration, for the shortest possible time, should be used to achieve adequate oxygenation.

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses##### Adult and Paediatric
With the exception of conditions listed below, oxygen should generally be titrated to maintain SpO2 ≥94% (i.e, unless otherwise required, there is no need to push saturations to 100%). 

Deliver oxygen through a device appropriate to the required inspired concentration of oxygen.

- **Nasal cannulae: **1 – 4 litres/min (approx. 24 – 40% oxygen) for low oxygen requirements
- **Oxygen (‘Hudson’) mask: **6 – 15 litres/min (approx. 30 – 65% oxygen) for moderate oxygen requirements
- **Nebuliser: **8 litres/min for moderate oxygen requirements with need for aerosolised medications- **Non-rebreather mask (NRBM): **15 litres/min (approx. 60 – 97% oxygen) for high oxygen requirements- **Bag-valve-mask (BVM): **15 litres/min or demand valve (up to 100% oxygen) for high oxygen requirements- **Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP): **8 – 15 litres/min (titrated to achieve desired effect/pressure) for high oxygen requirements.
#### Conditions with specific oxygen requirement
- Conditions requiring 100% oxygen delivery (NRBM, BVM) 
- Obstetric emergencies
- Carbon monoxide poisoning
- Unrelieved upper airway obstruction
- Diving emergencies.
- COPD patients with possible CO2 retention – titrate oxygen delivery to maintain SpO2 of 88 – 92%.
- Rapid sequence intubation (RSI) – aim for highest SpO2 possible 
- High flow oxygen (15 litres/min) via nasal cannulae is mandatory during this procedure, in addition to IPPV.

#### Indications
- For patients that require supplemental oxygen.

#### Procedure

Figure 1

##### Nasal Prongs (minimal oxygen requirements – approx 24-40%)

- Attach nasal prong tubing to the oxygen port.

- Select oxygen flow rate. 
- **Note: 15 Lpm continuous during intubation.**

- Place one nasal prong in each nostril and tubing tucked behind each ear.

- Position the slide adjuster underneath the patient’s chin.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.

Figure 2

##### Hudson Mask (moderate oxygen requirements – approx 30-65%)

- Attach the oxygen tubing to the Hudson mask.

- Connect the oxygen tubing to the oxygen port.

- Place elastic banding over patients head and adjust as required.

- Position the mask over the patient’s nose and mouth, moulding the metal nose piece if required.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.

Figure 3

##### Non-Rebreather Mask (high oxygen requirements - approx 60-97%)

- Prepare as for Hudson mask.

- Select oxygen flow rate of >10 Lpm.

- Ensure reservoir bag is inflated.

- Place elastic banding over patients head and adjust as required.

- Position the mask over the patient’s nose and mouth, moulding the nose piece if required.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.

Atropine.text: In an MCI situation, it’s permissible to simply repeat the initial atropine dose every 5/60.
If heart rate less than 50/minute, consider atropine prior to suxamethonium.

##### Type
Parasympathetic blocking agent [S4]

##### Presentations
1200mcg in 1ml – plastic ampoule 

##### Actions

- Blocks the action of the vagus nerve on the heart

- Increases the rate of the sinus node

- Increases speed of conduction through the AV node

- Reduces the amount of secretions from some glands (e.g., tear and salivary glands)

Onset IV: 2 minutes. Max effect: 5 minutes.

IV half-life: adult – 2–3 hours; paed – approx. 6.5 hours.

#### Uses

- Bradyarrhythmias with poor perfusion **- ICP only**

- Organophosphate/ nerve agent poisoning

- Big black spider bite with cholinergic symptoms

#### Adverse Effects

- Tachycardia

- Palpitations

- Blurred vision

- Dry mouth

- Confusion

- Urinary retention

- Increased body temperature (by decreasing perspiration)

#### Contraindications
Known hypersensitivity.

#### Precautions

- Care needed in patients with glaucoma

- Aim not to increase heart rate above 100bpm

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses#### Bradyarrhythmias##### Adult
- **ICP:** IV / IO: 600mcg – fast push 
- Repeat once after 3-5 minutes, if required.
##### Paediatric
Not used

#### Organophosphate / Nerve Agent Poisoning##### Adult
- IV / IO / IM: initial dose 2400mcg – fast push
- **AP: ** IM/IV: initial dose 2400mcg – fast push
- Repeat every 3 – 5 minutes.
- Double each repeat dose as required (e.g. 2.4mg → 4.8mg → 9.6mg etc.). - No upper limit on doses until all management end goals achieved.

- **Note:** Give IM while IV/IO access is being obtained.
##### Paediatric
- IV / IO / IM: initial dose 50mcg/kg (max. initial dose 2400mcg) – fast push 
- **AP: ** IM/IV: initial dose 50mcg/kg (max. initial dose 2400mcg) – fast push - Repeat every 3 – 5 minutes.
- Double each repeat dose as required- No upper limit on doses until all management end goals achieved.

- **Note:** Give IM while IV/IO access is being obtained.
- Split larger doses into multiple injections. 
**Note:** In an MCI situation where multiple patients require simultaneous management, a simplified repeat dose regimen, where the initial dose is repeated instead of doubled doses, can be used. 

#### Big black spider bite with cholinergic symptoms##### Adult
- IV / IO / IM: 600mcg – fast push
- **AP: ** IM/IV: 600mcg – fast push
- No repeat##### Paediatric
- IV / IO / IM: 20mcg/kg (max. 600mcg) – fast push
- **AP: ** IM/IV: 20mcg/kg (max. 600mcg) - fast- No repeat
